thi3:1.0.39: In the twenty-five yearssince I went forth,I don’t know that I had ever foundserenity in my mind.
thi3:1.0.40: I had failed to find peace of heart,or any control over my mind.When I remembered the victor’s instructions,I was struck with a sense of urgency.
thi3:1.0.41: Though subject to so many painful things,I have, through my love of diligence,reached the ending of craving,and fulfilled the Buddha’s instructions.This is the seventh daysince my craving dried up.
thi3:1.0.42: Four or five timesI left my dwelling.I had failed to find peace of heart,or any control over my mind.
thi3:1.0.43: I approached a nunin whom I had faith.She taught me the Dhamma:the aggregates, sense fields, and elements.
thi3:1.0.44: When I had heard her teaching,in accordance with her instructions,I sat cross-legged for seven days without moving,given over to rapture and bliss.On the eighth day I stretched out my feet,having shattered the mass of darkness.
thi3:1.0.45: Of the seven awakening factors,the path for attaining extinguishment,I have developed them all,just as the Buddha taught.
thi3:1.0.46: I attain the meditations on emptinessand signlessness whenever I want.I am the Buddha’s rightful daughter,always delighting in quenching.
thi3:1.0.47: All sensual pleasures are cut off,whether human or divine.Transmigration through births is finished,now there’ll be no more future lives.
thi3:1.0.48: Leaving my day’s meditationon Vulture’s Peak Mountain,I saw an elephant on the riverbankhaving just come up from his bath.
thi3:1.0.49: A man, taking a pole with a hook,asked the elephant: “Give me your foot.”The elephant presented his foot,and the man mounted him.
thi3:1.0.50: Seeing a wild beast so tamed,submitting to human control,my mind became serene:thatis why I’ve gone to the forest!
thi3:1.0.51: “You cry ‘Please be living!’ in the forest.Ubbirī, get a hold of yourself!Eighty-four thousand people,all named ‘living being’,have been burnt in this funeral ground:which one do you grieve for?”
thi3:1.0.52: “Oh! For you have plucked the arrow from me,so hard to see, stuck in the heart.You’ve swept away the grief for my daughterin which I once was mired.
thi3:1.0.53: Today I’ve plucked the arrow,I’m hungerless, extinguished.I go for refuge to that sage, the Buddha,to his teaching, and to the Saṅgha.”
thi3:1.0.54: “What’s up with these people in Rājagaha?They sprawl like they’ve been drinking mead!They don’t attend on Sukkāas she teaches the Buddha’s instructions.
thi3:1.0.55: But the wise—it’s as if they drink it up,so irresistible, delicious and nutritious,like travelers enjoying a cool cloud.”
thi3:1.0.56: “She’s known as Sukkā because of her bright qualities,free of greed, serene.She bears her final body,having vanquished Māra and his mount.”
thi3:1.0.57: “There’s no escape in the world,so what will seclusion do for you?Enjoy the delights of sensual pleasure;don’t regret it later.”
thi3:1.0.58: “Sensual pleasures are like swords and stakesthe aggregates are their chopping block.What you call sensual delightis now no delight for me.
thi3:1.0.59: Relishing is destroyed in every respect,and the mass of darkness is shattered.So know this, Wicked One:you’re beaten, terminator!”
thi3:1.0.60: “That state’s very challenging;it’s for the sages to attain.It’s not possible for a woman,with her two-fingered wisdom.”
thi3:1.0.61: “What difference does womanhood makewhen the mind is serene,and knowledge is presentas you rightly discern the Dhamma.
thi3:1.0.62: Relishing is destroyed in every respect,and the mass of darkness is shattered.So know this, Wicked One:you’re beaten, terminator!”
